title-group.article-title: Interpretable machine learning for predicting low-dose methylprednisolone effectiveness in long COVID

contrib-group.aff: 1The Third Affiliated Hospital of Zhejiang Chinese Medical University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China
2The First Hospital of Jiaxing, Jiaxing, Zhejiang, China
4The First Clinical Medical College of Zhejiang Chinese Medical University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.title: Summary
abstract.p: Long COVID is a chronic, multisystem disease with limited response to conventional treatments. While low-dose methylprednisolone has shown effectiveness in some patients, individual responses vary, and accurate predictive tools are lacking. This retrospective study included 330 long COVID patients who received low-dose methylprednisolone treatment across three hospitals. Patients were divided into training (n = 202), test (n = 33), and external validation sets (n = 53, n = 42). Using least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) regression, 38 variables were analyzed to develop six machine learning models. The logistic regression (LR) model showed stable performance across all datasets (AUCs: 0.8715, 0.7198, 0.8419, and 0.8676), making it the final model selected. SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis identified seven key variables, which were used to construct a nomogram for predicting treatment efficacy. The LR model and nomogram demonstrated strong predictive performance and clinical interpretability, offering a valuable decision-support tool for individualized treatment of long COVID with low-dose methylprednisolone.

list-item.p: Predictive model for low-dose methylprednisolone in long COVID treatment
Multi-center data used to train, test, and validate multiple models for reliability
Identified key clinical features influencing treatment outcomes
Created an interpretable model for personalized treatment decisions in long COVID

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Long COVID refers to a syndrome characterized by persistent or newly developed symptoms lasting for three months or more after SARS-CoV-2 infection, with notable heterogeneity and a tendency for chronicity.1,2 These symptoms affect multiple systems,3 among which chronic cough, dyspnea, persistent fatigue, and neuropsychiatric symptoms are particularly persistent. Conventional treatments often show limited efficacy, significantly affecting patients’ quality of life and presenting substantial challenges to clinical management.4,5 Therefore, identifying effective and reliable interventions has become a key focus of current research and clinical practice.

The various symptoms of long COVID may result from the systemic inflammation and immune dysregulation triggered by SARS-CoV-2 infection.6 Methylprednisolone, a synthetic corticosteroid, has been shown to effectively treat diseases suspected to have autoimmune etiology, exhibiting potent anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects.7 Studies indicate that low-dose methylprednisolone has demonstrated some efficacy in long COVID patients.8,9 Swiss guidelines for long COVID recommend the use of empirical inhaled steroids to treat chronic cough post long COVID,10 while Chinese guidelines recommend corticosteroids (including methylprednisolone) for treatment.11 However, the evidence supporting methylprednisolone’s use in long COVID is still limited,11 with clinicians often relying on experience to guide the treatment. Thus, there is an urgent need for a precise diagnostic and therapeutic framework to guide the use of methylprednisolone in long COVID.

Machine learning (ML), a key branch of artificial intelligence, has been widely applied in clinical research, especially in areas such as efficacy evaluation, disease risk prediction, and diagnostic assistance.12 Compared to traditional statistical methods, ML can automatically learn latent patterns from high-dimensional, multivariable, and complex nonlinear data, identifying hidden interactions and nonlinear relationships between variables, thus enhancing the model’s predictive performance and generalizability.13 With the development of interpretable ML models, models are no longer “black boxes.” Researchers can utilize methods such as SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and local interpretable model-agnostic explanations (LIME) to quantitatively demonstrate the specific contributions of each variable to the model’s predictions, thereby enhancing the clinical interpretability and reliability of the model.14 Given these advantages, ML can serve as a powerful tool for exploring and predicting the efficacy of corticosteroid treatment for long COVID, providing methodological support for developing more precise and individualized treatment strategies.

In this study, we developed a predictive model for the efficacy of low-dose methylprednisolone in long COVID treatment using six ML algorithms, based on patient data recruited from the Third Affiliated Hospital of Zhejiang Chinese Medical University (TAHZCMU) between 2022 and 2024. The model was tested internally, followed by external validation using data from patients recruited at the First Hospital of Jiaxing (FHJ) and Haining People’s Hospital (HPH) from 2023 to 2024. We aim to provide clinicians with a decision-support tool for the individualized, precise application of methylprednisolone in long COVID treatment.

sec.title: Feature selection and results of the least absolute shrinkage and selection operator regression
sec.p:
We first performed receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis on the individual characteristics to assess their discriminative ability (Figure 1A). Additionally, a bar chart reflecting the area under the curve (AUC) was plotted to visually display the predictive performance of each variable (Figure 1B). The results showed that the following features had relatively high AUC values: C-reactive protein (CRP) (AUC = 0.724), short-form (36) health survey (SF-36) (AUC = 0.722), neutrophil percentage (NEUTP) (AUC = 0.692), interleukin-6 (IL-6) (AUC = 0.689), Ratio of forced expiratory volume in 1 second to forced vital capacity (FEV1/FVC) (AUC = 0.674), Borg dyspnea scale after 6-minute walk test (6MWT as Borg2) (AUC = 0.656), Borg dyspnea scale before 6MWT (as Borg1) (AUC = 0.612), fatigue (AUC = 0.594), Pittsburgh sleep quality index (PSQI) (AUC = 0.593), and aversion to cold (AC) (AUC = 0.571). These variables were considered as potential model candidates. To better select variables, we performed a correlation analysis on all features (Figure 1C).Figure 1
The results of the ROC curve, AUC value, and correlation analysis
(A) Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve plot; (B) area under the curve (AUC) bar chart; (C) correlation matrix heatmap. AC, aversion to cold; BMI, body mass index; Borg1, Borg dyspnea scale before 6MWT; Borg2, Borg dyspnea scale after 6MWT; CRP, C-reactive protein; CT, chest tightness; FEV1/FVC, ratio of forced expiratory volume in 1 second to forced vital capacity; IL-6, interleukin-6; LYMP, lymphocyte percentage; MONOP, monocyte percentage; NC, nasal congestion; NEUTP, neutrophil percentage; PLT, platelet; PSQI, Pittsburgh sleep quality index; RBC, red blood cell; RI, recurrent infection; SF-36, short-form (36) health survey; 6MWT, six-minute walk test; SNS, spontaneous/night sweats; ST, sore throat; STP, smell or taste problem; WBC, white blood cell. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

Based on this, we employed a 10-fold cross-validation method to determine the optimal regularization parameter (lambda) in LASSO regression. The optimal value was represented by the lowest standard and the lowest standard plus one standard error, denoted by a dashed line (Figures 2A and 2B). Ultimately, LASSO regression selected seven statistically significant important variables: fatigue, SF-36, FEV1/FVC, PSQI, IL-6, CRP, and NEUTP. Given that the correlation between these variables was not strong, all were included in the final predictive model for ML training.Figure 2
The results of LASSO regression
(A) LASSO coefficient path plot; (b) cross-validation error plot for LASSO. The mean cross-validated error is plotted against log(λ). The error bars represent the mean ± one standard error (mean ± SEM) of the cross-validated error across the 10 folds. LASSO, the least absolute shrinkage and selection operator; AC, aversion to cold; BMI, body mass index; Borg1, Borg dyspnea scale before 6MWT; Borg2, Borg dyspnea scale after 6MWT; CRP, C-reactive protein; CT, chest tightness; FEV1/FVC, ratio of forced expiratory volume in 1 second to forced vital capacity; IL-6, interleukin-6; LYMP, lymphocyte percentage; MONOP, monocyte percentage; NC, nasal congestion; NEUTP, neutrophil percentage; PLT, platelet; PSQI, Pittsburgh sleep quality index; RBC, red blood cell; RI, recurrent infection; SF-36, short-form (36) health survey; 6MWT, six-minute walk test; SNS, spontaneous/night sweats; ST, sore throat; STP, smell or taste problem; WBC, white blood cell.

sec.title: Model evaluation
sec.p:
When evaluating the performance of ML models in the training set, the extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) model performed the best, achieving the highest AUC value (0.9807, 95% CI, 0.9648–0.9965) (Figure 3A; Table 2). Additionally, considering the imbalance in the dataset, we further assessed model performance using the F1 score. The F1 score provides a balanced measure of precision (positive predictive value) and recall (sensitivity), making it suitable for such scenarios.15,16 The XGBoost model achieved the highest F1 score (0.9551, 95% CI, 0.9301–0.9761). However, in the test set, XGBoost showed a significant decline in performance, with an AUC of 0.6154 (95% CI, 0.3983–0.(8325) and an F1 score of 0.7818 (95% CI, 0.6364–0.(8929) (Table 3; Figure 3B), possibly due to overfitting during the training process, which led to insufficient generalization.17 To solve this problem, we optimized the hyperparameters of the XGBoost model through the grid search method. The model was tuned for key hyperparameters such as learning rate (eta), maximum depth (max_depth), minimum child weight (min_child_weight), subsample ratio, and colsample_bytree. Although it achieved a relatively high accuracy rate (95.54%) on the training set, the performance of this model on the test set dropped significantly (69.70%), indicating that overfitting still exists. In contrast, the logistic regression (LR) model demonstrated more stable performance across the training set and test set with minimal fluctuations in AUC and F1 score, and overall excellent performance (Tables 2 and 3; Figures 3A and 3B). Therefore, the LR model was selected for downstream analysis. Additionally, the external validation set was used to assess the robustness of the final selected model (Tables 4 and 5; Figures 3C and 3D), and the LR model maintained stable performance, validating its effectiveness.Figure 3
Combined ROC curves of multiple machine learning models in different cohorts
(A) Combined ROC curves of TAHZCMU training cohort; (B) combined ROC curves of TAHZCMU test cohort; (C) combined ROC curves of FHJ validation cohort; (D) combined ROC curves of HPH validation cohort. ROC, receiver operating characteristic curve; TAHZCMU, The Third Affiliated Hospital of Zhejiang Chinese Medical University; FHJ, The First Hospital of Jiaxing; HPH, Haining people’s Hospital; XGBoost, extreme gradient boosting; NN, neural network; RF, random forest; SVM, support vector machine; KNN, K-nearest neighbors; LR, logistic regression.Table 2
Evaluation of model performance in TAHZCMU training cohort
AUC (95% CI)Accuracy (95% CI)F1 score (95% CI)Sensitivity (95% CI)Specificity (95% CI)
XGBoost0.9807 (0.9648,0.9965)0.9257 (0.8805,0.9578)0.9551 (0.9301,0.9761)0.9815 (0.9429,0.9952)0.6923 (0.5227,0.8245)
NN0.8798 (0.8242,0.9354)0.8366 (0.7783,0.8848)0.8949 (0.8599,0.9279)0.8589 (0.7937,0.9066)0.7436 (0.5757,0.8640)
RF0.9520 (0.9193,0.9848)0.9059 (0.8570,0.9424)0.9387 (0.9091,0.9639)0.9018 (0.8429,0.9411)0.9231 (0.7803,0.9799)
SVM0.9261 (0.8851,0.9670)0.8614 (0.8059,0.9059)0.9086 (0.8718,0.9415)0.8528 (0.7868,0.9016)0.8974 (0.7484,0.9666)
KNN0.9066 (0.8637,0.9494)0.7772 (0.7135,0.8326)0.8426 (0.7958,0.8897)0.7423 (0.6669,0.8061)0.9231 (0.7803,0.9799)
LR0.8715 (0.8093,0.9337)0.8614 (0.8059,0.9059)0.9165 (0.8847,0.9471)0.9448 (0.8946,0.9728)0.5128 (0.3503,0.6729)
Data are shown as n (95% CI); sensitivity = TP/(TP + FN); specificity = TN/(TN + FP); accuracy = (TP + TN)/(TP + TN + FP + FN); F1 = (2 ∗TP)/(2 ∗TP + FP + FN). FN, false negative; FP, false positive; TN, true negative; TP, true positive. TAHZCMU, The Third Affiliated Hospital of Zhejiang Chinese Medical University; AUC, area under the curve; XGBoost, extreme gradient boosting; NN, neural network; RF, random forest; SVM, support vector machine; KNN, K-nearest neighbors; LR, logistic regression.Table 3
Evaluation of model performance in TAHZCMU test cohort
AUC (95% CI)Accuracy (95% CI)F1 score (95% CI)Sensitivity (95% CI)Specificity (95% CI)
XGBoost0.6154 (0.3983,0.8325)0.6667 (0.4817,0.8204)0.7818 (0.6364,0.8929)0.7692 (0.5592,0.9025)0.2857 (0.0511,0.6974)
NN0.7253 (0.4914,0.9591)0.6660 (0.4848,0.8182)0.7514 (0.5789,0.8750)0.6538 (0.4436,0.8205)0.7143 (0.3026,0.9489)
RF0.6044 (0.3473,0.8615)0.3939 (0.2291,0.5786)0.4668 (0.2665,0.6522)0.3462 (0.1794,0.5564)0.5714 (0.2024,0.8819)
SVM0.6044 (0.3426,0.8662)0.4545 (0.2811,0.6365)0.5155 (0.3030,0.6977)0.3846 (0.2091,0.5927)0.7143 (0.3026,0.9489)
KNN0.5852 (0.3521,0.8182)0.4848 (0.3080,0.6646)0.5744 (0.3529,0.7442)0.4615 (0.2713,0.6625)0.5714 (0.2024,0.8819)
LR0.7198 (0.4989,0.9406)0.7576 (0.5774,0.8891)0.8463 (0.7390,0.9434)0.8846 (0.6872,0.9697)0.2857 (0.0511,0.6974)
Data are shown as n (95% CI); sensitivity = TP/(TP + FN); specificity = TN/(TN + FP); accuracy = (TP + TN)/(TP + TN + FP + FN); F1 = (2 ∗TP)/(2 ∗TP + FP + FN). FN, false negative; FP, false positive; TN, true negative; TP, true positive. TAHZCMU, The Third Affiliated Hospital of Zhejiang Chinese Medical University; AUC, area under the curve; XGBoost, extreme gradient boosting; NN, neural network; RF, random forest; SVM, support vector machine; KNN, K-nearest neighbors; LR, logistic regression.Table 4
Evaluation of model performance in FHJ validation cohort
AUC (95% CI)Accuracy (95% CI)F1 score (95% CI)Sensitivity (95% CI)Specificity (95% CI)
XGBoost0.8395 (0.6976,0.9815)0.8113 (0.6803,0.9056)0.8816 (0.7944,0.9485)0.8605 (0.7137,0.9420)0.6000 (0.2737,0.8631)
NN0.8349 (0.6735,0.9963)0.7358 (0.5967,0.8474)0.8108 (0.7042,0.9000)0.6977 (0.5370,0.8233)0.9000 (0.5412,0.9948)
RF0.8512 (0.7327,0.9696)0.5660 (0.4228,0.7016)0.6331 (0.4815,0.7536)0.4651 (0.3147,0.6217)1.0000 (0.6555,1.0000)
SVM0.8372 (0.7160,0.9584)0.5283 (0.3864,0.6670)0.5829 (0.4313,0.7213)0.4186 (0.2737,0.5779)1.0000 (0.6555,1.0000)
KNN0.7814 (0.6360,0.9268)0.6038 (0.4600,0.7355)0.6851 (0.5424,0.8052)0.5349 (0.3783,0.6853)0.9000 (0.5412,0.9948)
LR0.8419 (0.6986,0.9851)0.8491 (0.7241,0.9325)0.9078 (0.8430,0.9680)0.9302 (0.7988,0.9818)0.5000 (0.2014,0.7986)

XGBoost0.7500 (0.5503,0.9497)0.8333 (0.6864,0.9303)0.9067 (0.8286,0.9620)1.0000 (0.8736,1.0000)0.1250 (0.0066,0.5332)

HPH, Haining people’s Hospital; AUC, area under the curve; XGBoost, extreme gradient boosting; NN, neural network; RF, random forest; SVM, support vector machine; KNN, K-nearest neighbors; LR, logistic regression.

sec.title: Interpretation of ML models
sec.p:
Based on the results of LR model, a corresponding nomogram was constructed (Figure 4). The nomogram illustrates the contribution of variables such as fatigue, SF-36, FEV1/FVC, PSQI, IL-6, CRP, and NEUTP to predicting treatment efficacy. Each variable is represented by a scale, and the corresponding score (points) can be obtained based on its specific value. The total score is then calculated by summing the scores of all variables, which is mapped to the “total points” scale, and subsequently translated to the “linear predictor” and “efficacy rate” scales, providing a visual representation of the predicted probability for an individual.Figure 4
Nomogram
SF-36, short-form (36) health survey; FEV1/FVC, ratio of forced expiratory volume in 1 second to forced vital capacity; PSQI, Pittsburgh sleep quality index; IL-6, interleukin-6; CRP, C-reactive protein; NEUTP, neutrophil percentage.

The SHAP feature importance of LR model is shown in Figure 5A, where the features are ranked from highest to lowest based on their average absolute SHAP values. The key features identified were NEUTP, SF-36, IL-6, Fatigue, CRP, FEV1/FVC, and PSQI. The SHAP summary plot (Figure 5B) illustrates the impact of each feature on the model’s prediction. Higher SHAP values indicate a stronger association with the effectiveness of methylprednisolone treatment for long COVID. For example, patients with higher levels of CRP and IL-6 are associated with a better therapeutic response, while those with lower levels of CRP and IL-6 do not have this correlation. Similarly, patients with fatigue symptoms have a stronger association with treatment effectiveness compared to those without fatigue symptoms.Figure 5
SHAP value of each feature in the model
(A) SHAP feature importance shown according to the mean absolute SHAP value of each feature; (B) SHAP summary plot showing the distribution of the SHAP values of each feature. NEUTP, neutrophil percentage; SF-36, short-form (36) health survey; IL-6, interleukin-6; CRP, C-reactive protein; FEV1/FVC, ratio of forced expiratory volume in 1 second to forced vital capacity; PSQI, Pittsburgh sleep quality index.

Additionally, the SHAP-dependence plots reveal the specific impact of each continuous variable on the prediction of treatment efficacy with low-dose methylprednisolone (Figure 6). Lower values of SF-36, FEV1/FVC, and PSQI (x axis) were associated with higher SHAP values (y axis) (Figures 6A–6C). In contrast, as IL-6, CRP, and NEUTP values (x axis) increased, the SHAP values (y axis) also rose (Figures 6D–6F). An increase in SHAP values is associated with a greater improvement in treatment outcomes for long COVID patients (y axis values increase).Figure 6
SHAP dependence plots of continuous features in the model
The y axis shows the SHAP values corresponding to each feature, while the x axis indicates the values of specific features. Continuous variables were normalized using the min-max scaling technique, which transformed them into a range between 0 and 1. Each point on the plot represents a SHAP value for a particular feature for an individual patient. SF-36, short-form (36) health survey; FEV1/FVC, ratio of forced expiratory volume in 1 second to forced vital capacity; PSQI, Pittsburgh sleep quality index; IL-6, interleukin-6; CRP, C-reactive protein; NEUTP, neutrophil percentage.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Long COVID manifests as a series of debilitating symptoms, and there is currently no specific treatment, particularly in patients with persistent inflammation and immune dysregulation.6 Methylprednisolone, a corticosteroid, has shown good anti-inflammatory effects and improves pulmonary compliance when applied at low doses.18,19 Previous studies have preliminarily confirmed that low-dose methylprednisolone can improve symptoms in some long COVID patients, especially in areas such as fatigue and respiratory function.8 However, individual treatment responses vary and there is a lack of effective tools to predict which patients will benefit. To better facilitate the application of methylprednisolone in long COVID treatment, this study is the first to integrate clinical data from multiple hospitals and systematically assess predictive factors for the efficacy of low-dose methylprednisolone treatment using six mainstream ML models.

In model performance comparisons, XGBoost demonstrated high fitting capabilities in the training set, showing initial good results. However, the model performed poorly on the test set. Despite hyperparameter tuning, overfitting remains an issue. This could be due to the complexity of the model, particularly when the tree depth is large and the number of estimators is high.20 In contrast, the LR model performed well across the training set, test set, and external validation sets, reflecting better stability and overall predictive performance. Therefore, we consider the LR model the most suitable predictive model in this study.

LR models model the association between features and outcomes based on linear relationships. Their parameter estimation process is transparent, and the results are easily interpretable, clearly reflecting each feature’s independent contribution to the outcome variable.21 Furthermore, LR models use L1/L2 regularization to constrain weights, suppress noise feature interference, and enhance generalization ability, making them less prone to overfitting.22 Additionally, the regression coefficients and probability thresholds generated by the LR model are easy for clinicians to understand, allowing them to develop personalized treatment plans based on thresholds, thus avoiding the arbitrariness of absolute classifications and aiding in converting model results into actionable clinical decisions.23,24 Therefore, the comprehensive stability, interpretability, and probabilistic output capabilities of LR make it a more reliable choice compared to complex ensemble models.

Before analyzing the ML models, we used LASSO regression to select seven representative variables to construct the predictive model. We then used SHAP values to analyze the importance of each variable in the model’s prediction, revealing their key roles in assessing the efficacy of low-dose methylprednisolone in treating long COVID. The seven core features identified were fatigue, SF-36, FEV1/FVC, PSQI, IL-6, CRP, and NEUTP, all of which are easily accessible and assessable in clinical settings and have high practical application value.

Fatigue is a common symptom of long COVID and is closely related to chronic low-grade inflammation, immune system dysregulation, neuroendocrine axis dysfunction, and cytokine imbalances. Research suggests that prolonged inflammation can cause neurotransmitter metabolic disorders in the central nervous system, leading to persistent fatigue.25 Additionally, dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis during post-infection recovery may lead to cortisol imbalance, further exacerbating immune responses and fatigue.26 This feature suggests that low-dose methylprednisolone may play a significant role in alleviating fatigue associated with immune inflammation, neuroendocrine dysfunction, and psychological behavior disturbances.

SF-36 is a standardized questionnaire reflecting overall health status and effectively assesses the quality of life and health conditions in long COVID patients across multiple dimensions, including physical function, social function, and mental health.27 The inclusion of SF-36 as a key variable in the predictive model suggests that quality of life is an important reference in determining the treatment response in long COVID patients. Studies have shown that long COVID patients experience significant declines in quality of life, particularly in fatigue, mood, and social function, with improvements in these areas often being core components of patients’ perceived treatment efficacy.28 We observed that lower SF-36 scores were associated with higher probabilities of response to methylprednisolone treatment, suggesting that patients with more severely impaired initial quality of life might benefit more from the anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects of low-dose methylprednisolone.

FEV1/FVC is a classic indicator for evaluating lung function, reflecting airway resistance and pulmonary ventilation capacity. Its decrease typically indicates obstructive ventilatory dysfunction.29 In long COVID patients, persistent lung inflammation, interstitial changes, and airway remodeling following infection can lead to impaired lung function, manifested as a reduction in FEV1/FVC.30 This suggests that low-dose methylprednisolone, by modulating inflammatory responses, may improve lung ventilation function in long COVID patients, thereby alleviating related respiratory symptoms and enhancing overall functional status.

PSQI is a commonly used scale for evaluating sleep disorders, primarily reflecting subjective sleep quality, sleep latency, sleep time, sleep efficiency, sleep disturbances, hypnotic drug use, and daytime function over the past month. Lower PSQI scores indicate better sleep quality.31 However, in our predictive model, higher PSQI scores corresponded to lower SHAP values, suggesting that patients with more severe sleep disorders might respond less favorably to low-dose methylprednisolone treatment, or even experience some negative effects. This result may be related to corticosteroid interference with the central nervous system. Previous studies have shown that corticosteroids can activate the HPA axis, increase nighttime cortisol levels, disrupt normal circadian rhythms, and exacerbate pre-existing sleep disorders.32 Moreover, corticosteroids may inhibit melatonin secretion, thereby affecting sleep quality.33 Therefore, in long COVID patients with significant sleep disturbances, methylprednisolone treatment should be approached more cautiously, possibly in combination with sleep interventions or adjusted dosing times to reduce potential negative impacts on sleep.

IL-6 is a key factor in reflecting inflammatory responses and immune activation. In long COVID, IL-6 may contribute to symptom persistence through sustained immune activation and chronic inflammation.34 CRP reflects systemic inflammation, and existing studies indicate that elevated CRP levels are closely associated with the occurrence of long COVID, possibly driving symptom persistence through prolonged low-grade inflammation.35 NEUTP reflects neutrophil activity and immune response, and in long COVID, elevated NEUTP is associated with excessive immune responses and potential bacterial infections following infection.36 These biomarkers, as key features in the model, suggest that low-dose methylprednisolone can alleviate symptoms and improve clinical outcomes in long COVID by suppressing inflammation and modulating immune function. While these mechanisms require further verification through rigorous biological studies, their preliminary value in predicting treatment efficacy has already been established.

Therefore, although the pathogenesis of long COVID is not fully understood and treatment strategies are still evolving, the key features identified by the ML model in this study effectively reflect underlying pathological processes, providing theoretical support and predictive reference for the application of low-dose methylprednisolone in long COVID patients. The occurrence of increased intraocular pressure in two patients was noted during the study. This side effect is consistent with prior reports of corticosteroid treatment but was reversible upon discontinuation. Future studies should focus on better monitoring and reporting of adverse events to ensure the long-term safety of low-dose methylprednisolone in the treatment of long COVID. Additionally, feature identification within the ML model not only aids in pre-screening potential benefit populations for clinical practice but also provides directions for subsequent research and optimization of corticosteroid treatment strategies. This offers new insights for addressing the multisystem pathological changes associated with long COVID.

To further enhance the clinical operability and individualized guidance capability of the model, we constructed a nomogram based on the LR model in addition to the ML model, serving as a useful supplementary tool. The nomogram assigns corresponding scores to each variable in the regression model and visually displays the relationship between the variables and prognosis risk. When the model’s predicted value exceeds the baseline prediction, and the nomogram’s efficacy rate is >0.999, it indicates a higher likelihood that long COVID patients will benefit from low-dose methylprednisolone treatment. Clinicians can quickly obtain individualized risk assessments based on specific patient characteristics, allowing for the development of more precise treatment strategies. Moreover, the nomogram helps enhance patients’ understanding of treatment risks and benefits, thereby improving their comprehension of the treatment’s necessity and adherence.37

In summary, this study, based on multicenter retrospective data, developed and validated an interpretable ML model for predicting the efficacy of low-dose methylprednisolone in treating long COVID. The results show that the LR model demonstrated stable performance across the training set, test set, and external validation sets, with good predictive performance and clinical interpretability. The seven key variables selected (fatigue, SF-36, FEV1/FVC, PSQI, IL-6, CRP, NEUTP) demonstrated significant predictive value in the model. SHAP analysis further revealed the impact of these variables on the model’s predictions. The nomogram constructed based on the LR model provides clinicians with a visual, individualized assessment tool that helps identify patients who may benefit from treatment, facilitating more precise treatment decisions. This study provides a theoretical foundation and practical support for the individualized application of low-dose methylprednisolone in long COVID.

sec.title: Limitations of the study
sec.p: This study has several limitations: (1) although data from multiple hospitals were used, all the research sites were located in the same geographical area, and the recruitment process was limited to 2022 to 2024, with the follow-up period limited to 14 days, moreover, the two external validation cohorts were modest in size. These factors may affect the model’s stability and generalizability; (2) retrospective data are prone to confounding bias. Although a comparative analysis of baseline characteristics was performed and sensitivity analysis was conducted to account for potential confounding factors in co-interventions, the potential residual confounding from unmeasured factors still poses a limitation; (3) this study did not consider potential time-related variables (e.g., symptom duration) or genetic factors, which could enhance the model’s predictive power in the future; and (4) while the F1 score was used to evaluate model performance, techniques such as re-balancing, threshold optimization, and cost-sensitive analysis were not applied. These methods, which could potentially improve model performance in imbalanced datasets, were not implemented due to the scope of this study. Furthermore, this study used a fixed low-dose methylprednisolone regimen, and the impact of a tapering strategy on efficacy and safety was not assessed. The effects of other corticosteroids or different dosages on the treatment of long COVID still require further research.

Future studies will recruit a larger sample size from multiple regions, extend the follow-up period, conduct sensitivity analyses, use alternative outcome indicators, to further verify the results, and update the model when necessary. Additionally, considering the inclusion of time-series features (e.g., dynamic changes in inflammatory biomarkers) or multimodal data (e.g., imaging and omics information) into the prediction framework could improve model performance. Moreover, integrating model outputs into an individualized treatment decision-support system could potentially enhance treatment efficiency and patient satisfaction for long COVID.

sec.title: Lead contact
sec.p: Further information and requests for resources should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Xianming Lin (linxianming1966@163.com).

sec.title: Materials availability
sec.p: No materials were used or generated in this study.

list-item.p: The datasets analyzed in this study are currently unavailable due to ethical approval constraints and data security considerations. The original data supporting the conclusions of this article are available from the authors upon request and will be provided without reservation.
The code for the machine learning model used in this study is available at Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.16777421.
Any additional information required to reanalyze the data reported in this article is available from the lead contact upon request.

sec.title: Acknowledgments
sec.p: This study was supported by the National and Provincial Joint Project (GZY-ZJ-KJ24023).

sec.title: Author contributions
sec.p: Jisheng Zhang, Y.C., and X.L. designed the study and the study concept. Jisheng Zhang, Y.C., A.Z., and Y.Y. designed the methodology. Jisheng Zhang, W.Z., and X.Y. designed the software. Jisheng Zhang, L.M., K.Y., Jiangsong Zhang, K.L., and X.L. performed validation. Jisheng Zhang and Y.C. conducted formal analysis. Jisheng Zhang, Y.C., A.Z., Y.Y., and Jiangsong Zhang performed the investigation. Jisheng Zhang, Y.C., A.Z., and Y.Y. performed data curation. Jisheng Zhang and Y.C. built the prediction model. Jisheng Zhang, L.M., K.Y., W.Z., and X.Y. designed the figures. Jisheng Zhang prepared the original draft. All authors have reviewed and approved this article. The authors declare no conflict of interest in relation to the submission of this manuscript.

sec.title: Declaration of interests
sec.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

sec.title: Experimental model and study participant details
sec.p: The data for this study were sourced from the Long COVID databases of the Third Affiliated Hospital of Zhejiang Chinese Medical University (TAHZCMU), the First Hospital of Jiaxing (FHJ), and Haining People’s Hospital (HPH). The objective of this study was to extract data from the database regarding patients treated with low-dose methylprednisolone for Long COVID and perform a retrospective analysis to develop and validate a predictive model for the efficacy of low-dose methylprednisolone treatment. The study protocol was approved by the TAHZCMU Ethics Committee (ZSLL-KY-2023-002-01). We adhered to the TRIPOD guidelines for transparent reporting of multivariable prediction models for individual prognosis or diagnosis.

A total of 407 Long COVID patients who received low-dose methylprednisolone treatment were extracted from the database. These patients were treated between December 2022 and February 2024 at TAHZCMU, FHJ, and HPH. The studies involving humans were approved by Ethics Committees of TAHZCMU, FHJ and HPH. The studies were conducted in accordance with the local legislation and institutional requirements. The participants provided their written informed consent to participate in this study. The detailed characteristic information and groupings of the patients can be found in Table 1.

We employed six machine learning (ML) models for the analysis: Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Neural Network (NN), Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Logistic Regression (LR).

sec.title: Data collection
sec.p: Based on the World Health Organization (WHO) guidelines and current clinical evidence, corticosteroids for patients with COVID are administered over a 7-10-day course during which the main clinical effects are achieved, and continued use beyond two weeks is not recommended.38 Meanwhile, short-term outpatient treatment leads to statistically and clinically significant improvements in physical function among patients with long COVID, and these effects persist over time.39 Therefore, all patients received methylprednisolone tablets (brand name: Medrol, manufactured by Pfizer, specification: 4mg/tablet) orally at a dose of 8 mg (2 tablets) daily for 2 consecutive weeks. Patients with the following conditions were excluded from the study: (1) no follow-up records; (2) more than 20% of clinical data missing in follow-up records; (3) treatment duration <14 days; (4) pregnant or breastfeeding women; (5) participants in other clinical drug trials; (6) patients who could not complete the treatment or trial due to other reasons. After excluding records with >20% missingness, we conducted a completeness review of all variables included in the analysis and found no remaining missing values (Table S1), therefore, no missing-data handling (including imputation) was performed.

Based on published studies, clinical expertise, and practical diagnostic factors, we selected potential efficacy-related factors from the case data of low-dose methylprednisolone treatment for Long COVID.7,9,40 For highly correlated factors, we retained the most relevant variables or combined them.41 Thus, this study collected the following factors: first, 9 baseline characteristics, including age, gender, body mass index (BMI), hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, smoking history, vaccination status, and recurrent infection; second, 21 clinical features and symptom scores: cough, expectoration, nasal congestion, chest tightness, insomnia, sore throat, fatigue, aversion to cold, headache, myalgia, palpitation, anorexia, spontaneous/night sweats, fever, smell/taste problems, Short Form (36) Health Survey (SF-36), Six-Minute Walk Test (6MWT), Borg Dyspnea Scale before 6MWT (Borg1), Borg Dyspnea Scale after 6MWT (Borg2), Ratio of Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 Second to Forced Vital Capacity (FEV1/FVC), and Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI). Third, 8 auxiliary examination indicators: Interleukin-6 (IL-6), C-reactive protein (CRP), white blood cells (WBC), red blood cells (RBC), platelets (PLT), neutrophil percentage (NEUTP), lymphocyte percentage (LYMP), and monocyte percentage (MONOP). Fourth, follow-up or feedback data after 14 days of treatment. All of the above factors were collected on the day of treatment initiation, and treatment also commenced on the same day.

As there are no standardized quantitative indicators to assess the improvement of COVID-19 long-term syndrome, symptom relief remains the primary measure of treatment efficacy.42 The World Health Organization (WHO) Long COVID guidelines indicate that treatment outcomes for Long COVID can be assessed by an experienced clinical team.43 SF-36 can comprehensively capture changes in both physiological and psychological functioning among patients with long COVID and thereby provides a broad characterization of treatment trajectories44; accordingly, it was selected as the primary efficacy indicator in this study. Drawing on prior research in long COVID and evidence from other disease areas regarding the Minimal Clinically Important Difference (MCID), we predefined an increase of >10 points in the SF-36 total score as the threshold for achieving the MCID and used this criterion to define treatment success.45,46

sec.title: Feature processing and selection
sec.p: A total of 38 features were incorporated into the model. Categorical data were processed as follows: gender was dichotomized (male = 1, female = 0), and binary indicators related to clinical manifestations, medical history, and reinfection were assigned values of 1 for affirmative responses and 0 otherwise. All continuous variables underwent min-max normalization using the standard formula: E((10) = (x–min)/(max–min).

To reduce confounding factors caused by treatment indications, we performed multivariable logistic regression to adjust for baseline characteristics and analyzed the standardized mean differences (SMDs) and odds ratios (ORs) of baseline features to assess baseline imbalances. No significant differences were observed (Table S2; Table S3), indicating the robustness of our model. Additionally, since the dataset does not include explicit co-intervention columns, we used symptoms (such as fatigue, cough, and insomnia) as reasonable proxies for potential co-interventions and conducted a sensitivity analysis by excluding patients with major co-interventions. The results of this analysis showed no statistically significant differences compared to the main analysis (Table S4), suggesting that the impact of co-interventions on treatment outcomes is minimal, allowing for subsequent analysis.

Variable selection was conducted using LASSO method. The objective function of LASSO regression introduces a penalty term to the least squares method, shrinking some regression coefficients to zero, thereby enabling efficient feature selection. Potential risk factors were identified through LASSO regression, and the nonzero features were subsequently included in both the model and the nomogram. To determine the optimal regularization parameter (lambda) for LASSO regression, we employed 10-fold cross-validation (CV). The optimal lambda was selected using the 1-standard error rule (1-SE rule), where the lambda with the lowest cross-validation error plus one standard error was chosen. Hyperparameter tuning was performed within the training data only, ensuring that no future data was used in the model selection process. This method helps prevent overfitting and ensures that the model is evaluated using appropriate data. LASSO regression was implemented using the glmnet package in R (version 4.4.2; Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria).

sec.title: Model construction and validation
sec.p: This study used patient data from TAHZCMU between December 2022 and February 2024 as the training and test sets, while patient data from FHJ and HPH between June 2023 and February 2024 were used as external validation sets. Six ML models were developed for analysis: XGBoost model was implemented using the XGBoost package in R, NN model was implemented using the nnet package in R, RF model was implemented using the randomForest package in R, SVM model was implemented using the e1071 package in R, KNN model was implemented using the kknn package in R, and LR model was implemented using the rms package in R. Model performance was evaluated based on AUC, sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and F1 score analysis. Among these, AUC and F1 score were the primary indicators for comparing model performance. The most suitable ML model was selected as the predictive model for this study, and the corresponding nomogram was constructed.

We applied SHAP method to explain the ML models and assess the impact of different features on the prediction outcomes, identifying key features in the process. SHAP effectively explained the model’s performance at both the cohort and individual patient levels. By calculating the SHAP value for each feature across all patients and averaging them, we were able to evaluate the contribution of each feature to the model. The SHAP feature importance plot demonstrated the global importance of these features, with higher mean absolute SHAP values indicating greater contributions to the model’s predictions. Additionally, SHAP dependence plots were created to further interpret the impact of specific features on the prediction results. The SHAP method was implemented using the iml and fastshap packages in R.

sec.title: Quantification and statistical analysis
sec.p: Continuous data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD) or median with interquartile range (IQR), while categorical data were presented as frequency and percentage. For comparisons between groups, categorical variables were analyzed using the chi-square test, and continuous data were assessed using an independent samples t test. All statistical analyses were conducted using IBM SPSS (Version 25.0; IBM, Armonk, NY, USA), with p < 0.05 (two-sided) considered statistically significant.

sec.title: Additional resources
sec.p: This study employed a retrospective analysis, and the data included in the database originated from a randomized controlled trial study, which was registered at the Chinese Clinical Trial Registry (Identifier: ChiCTR2400081131; Associated link: https://www.chictr.org.cn/showproj.html?proj=214400).
